case.title: Equation (1) definition and variable support
case.prompt: What does Equation (1) define in this paper, and where do the authors define the variables like t_n, t_f, sigma(r(t)), T(t), and c(r(t), d)? Point me to the exact supporting part.

contains_any: The variable definitions appear immediately after the equation:

case.paper_title: SCINeRF: Neural Radiance Fields from a Snapshot Compressive Image
case.source_path: F:\research-papers\2026\Jan\else\kb_chat\db\CVPR-2024-SCINeRF- Neural Radiance Fields from a Snapshot Compressive Image\CVPR-2024-SCINeRF- Neural Radiance Fields from a Snapshot Compressive Image.en.md
case.source_name: CVPR-2024-SCINeRF- Neural Radiance Fields from a Snapshot Compressive Image.pdf

payload.partial:
Equation (1) is stated in SCINeRF: Neural Radiance Fields from a Snapshot Compressive Image / 3. Method / 3.1. Background on NeRF:
The lead-in sentence says: Given a set of input multi-view images (together with both the camera intrinsic and extrinsic parameters), NeRF [26] transfers the pixels of the input images into rays. It then samples points along each ray, and takes 5D vectors (i.e., the 3D position of sampled point and the 2D viewing directions) as input. The volume density \sigma and view-dependent RGB color c of each sampled 3D point are then estimated by a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP). The reason that NeRF predicts color from both position and viewing direction is to better deal with the specular reflection of the scene. After obtaining the volume density and color of each sampled point along the ray, it employs a conventional volumetric rendering technique to integrate the density and color to synthesize the corresponding pixel intensity C of the image. The whole process can be formally defined via following equation:

$$
C(\mathbf{r}) = \int_{t_n}^{t_f} T(t)\sigma(\mathbf{r}(t))\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{r}(t),\mathbf{d})dt, \tag{1}
$$

The variable definitions appear immediately after the equation: where $t_n$ and $t_f$ are near and far bounds for volumetric rendering respectively, $r(t)$ is the sampled 3D point along the ray $r$ at the distance $t$ from the camera center, $\sigma(r(t))$ represents the predicted density of the sampled point $r(t)$ by the MLP, $T(t)$ denotes the accumulated transmittance along the ray from $t_n$ to $t$, and is defined as $\exp(-\int_{t_n}^{t} T(s)\sigma(r(s))ds)$, $d$ is the viewing direction in the world coordinate frame, and $c(r(t),d)$ is the predicted color of the sampled point $r(t)$ by the MLP.
payload.answer:
Equation (1) is stated in SCINeRF: Neural Radiance Fields from a Snapshot Compressive Image / 3. Method / 3.1. Background on NeRF:
The lead-in sentence says: Given a set of input multi-view images (together with both the camera intrinsic and extrinsic parameters), NeRF [26] transfers the pixels of the input images into rays. It then samples points along each ray, and takes 5D vectors (i.e., the 3D position of sampled point and the 2D viewing directions) as input. The volume density \sigma and view-dependent RGB color c of each sampled 3D point are then estimated by a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP). The reason that NeRF predicts color from both position and viewing direction is to better deal with the specular reflection of the scene. After obtaining the volume density and color of each sampled point along the ray, it employs a conventional volumetric rendering technique to integrate the density and color to synthesize the corresponding pixel intensity C of the image. The whole process can be formally defined via following equation:

$$
C(\mathbf{r}) = \int_{t_n}^{t_f} T(t)\sigma(\mathbf{r}(t))\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{r}(t),\mathbf{d})dt, \tag{1}
$$

The variable definitions appear immediately after the equation: where $t_n$ and $t_f$ are near and far bounds for volumetric rendering respectively, $r(t)$ is the sampled 3D point along the ray $r$ at the distance $t$ from the camera center, $\sigma(r(t))$ represents the predicted density of the sampled point $r(t)$ by the MLP, $T(t)$ denotes the accumulated transmittance along the ray from $t_n$ to $t$, and is defined as $\exp(-\int_{t_n}^{t} T(s)\sigma(r(s))ds)$, $d$ is the viewing direction in the world coordinate frame, and $c(r(t),d)$ is the predicted color of the sampled point $r(t)$ by the MLP.

The lead-in sentence says: Given a set of input multi-view images (together with both the camera intrinsic and extrinsic parameters), NeRF [26] transfers the pixels of the input images into rays. It then samples points along each ray, and takes 5D vectors (i.e., the 3D position of sampled point and the 2D viewing directions) as input. The volume density \sigma and view-dependent RGB color c of each sampled 3D point are then estimated by a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP). The reason that NeRF predicts color from both position and viewing direction is to better deal with the specular reflection of the scene. After obtaining the volume density and color of each sampled point along the ray, it employs a conventional volumetric rendering technique to integrate the density and color to synthesize the corresponding pixel intensity C of the image. The whole process can be formally defined via following equation:

$$
C(\mathbf{r}) = \int_{t_n}^{t_f} T(t)\sigma(\mathbf{r}(t))\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{r}(t),\mathbf{d})dt, \tag{1}

The variable definitions appear immediately after the equation: where $t_n$ and $t_f$ are near and far bounds for volumetric rendering respectively, $r(t)$ is the sampled 3D point along the ray $r$ at the distance $t$ from the camera center, $\sigma(r(t))$ represents the predicted density of the sampled point $r(t)$ by the MLP, $T(t)$ denotes the accumulated transmittance along the ray from $t_n$ to $t$, and is defined as $\exp(-\int_{t_n}^{t} T(s)\sigma(r(s))ds)$, $d$ is the viewing direction in the world coordinate frame, and $c(r(t),d)$ is the predicted color of the sampled point $r(t)$ by the MLP.

blk_29cad7662df5_00024.text: Given a set of input multi-view images (together with both the camera intrinsic and extrinsic parameters), NeRF [26] transfers the pixels of the input images into rays. It then samples points along each ray, and takes 5D vectors (i.e., the 3D position of sampled point and the 2D viewing directions) as input. The volume density \sigma and view-dependent RGB color c of each sampled 3D point are then estimated by a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP). The reason that NeRF predicts color from both position and viewing direction is to better deal with the specular reflection of the scene. After obtaining the volume density and color of each sampled point along the ray, it employs a conventional volumetric rendering technique to integrate the density and color to synthesize the corresponding pixel intensity C of the image. The whole process can be formally defined via following equation:

message.copy_markdown:
Equation (1) is stated in SCINeRF: Neural Radiance Fields from a Snapshot Compressive Image / 3. Method / 3.1. Background on NeRF:
The lead-in sentence says: Given a set of input multi-view images (together with both the camera intrinsic and extrinsic parameters), NeRF  transfers the pixels of the input images into rays. It then samples points along each ray, and takes 5D vectors (i.e., the 3D position of sampled point and the 2D viewing directions) as input. The volume density \sigma and view-dependent RGB color c of each sampled 3D point are then estimated by a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP). The reason that NeRF predicts color from both position and viewing direction is to better deal with the specular reflection of the scene. After obtaining the volume density and color of each sampled point along the ray, it employs a conventional volumetric rendering technique to integrate the density and color to synthesize the corresponding pixel intensity C of the image. The whole process can be formally defined via following equation:

$$
C(\mathbf{r}) = \int_{t_n}^{t_f} T(t)\sigma(\mathbf{r}(t))\mathbf{c}(\mathbf{r}(t),\mathbf{d})dt, \tag{1}
$$
*（式(1) 对应命中的库内文献：`CVPR-2024-SCINeRF- Neural Radiance Fields from a Snapshot Compressive Image.pdf`）*


The variable definitions appear immediately after the equation: where $t_n$ and $t_f$ are near and far bounds for volumetric rendering respectively, $r(t)$ is the sampled 3D point along the ray $r$ at the distance $t$ from the camera center, $\sigma(r(t))$ represents the predicted density of the sampled point $r(t)$ by the MLP, $T(t)$ denotes the accumulated transmittance along the ray from $t_n$ to $t$, and is defined as $\exp(-\int_{t_n}^{t} T(s)\sigma(r(s))ds)$, $d$ is the viewing direction in the world coordinate frame, and $c(r(t),d)$ is the predicted color of the sampled point $r(t)$ by the MLP.

It then samples points along each ray, and takes 5D vectors (i.e., the 3D position of sampled point and the 2D viewing directions) as input. The volume density \sigma and view-dependent RGB color c of each sampled 3D point are then estimated by a Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP).
